text: journey was a millisecond blip. So when my apartment flickered, superimposed over the surreal space I inhabited, I thought nothing of it. Until another heat began to build. A different kind of heat. The apartment came back again, and this time Vanessa’s ghost was riding me, bare chested and moaning. Her face twisted with pleasure. She held the back of my neck as she bucked. With her free hand, she filleted my arm. My living room disappeared, and I was floating in a multicolored miasma, surrounded by bulbous-eyed humanoids. The shock of their staring orbs caused me to gasp, sucking gelatin into my lungs. Then my eyes shot wide. The apartment returned, still only a transparent veil, but enough so I could breathe. Vanessa groaned, barely aware of her surroundings. Barely aware that I Mávri Pórta was freeing me from its grasp as she continued to cut and slice my arm. “Get it off!” Her eyes opened lazily, the smile of satisfaction loathe to flee. But she heard me. “Tear it off!” “Almost. There.” She covered my mouth with her hand. Oh, dear Christ. She covered my mouth with her bare hand. Where were the gloves? My words were lost behind her hand and between worlds as I tried to warn her. A golden orb appeared beneath my feet. I fell from a great height, waves of blue light swirling around me, the golden ground racing up. I threw my hands out bracing for impact, but when it came, I was on the plastic-covered floor of my apartment. She’d done it, she’d— I grabbed the Cambro and released the pent-up pressure inside, a flood of thick, living fluid, studded with gems of insight. The nectar of the gods. Ambrosia. Wiping my mouth, I looked to retrieve the sea creature. If it was still alive, we could preserve it for another trip. “He— I— Whe—” Vanessa was on the floor, her red hair spread around her like a puddle of blood. I Mávri Pórta was wrapped around her face and neck, tendrils extending over her naked body like spider webs. “No!” I ran to the chest and retrieved two scalpels and gloves. Vanessa faded completely out of sight, then pulsed back. I grabbed her arm to stabilize her and cut deep. Maybe too deep, but she was covered with the super organism’s filaments. I sliced again and blood spurt me in the face. I kept on cutting as Vanessa groaned and wriggled and screamed. The whole apartment stunk of sea and sex. My mouth was gritty. My vision was blurry from the trip. Blood was everywhere. Vanessa convulsed and my grip slipped. Then she slipped. Right out of our reality. Out of our world. I Mávri Pórta fell to the floor, squirming and satiated. Vanessa was gone. I fell back, panting, wiping blood from my face. “Not again.” # I didn’t sleep. There was too much clean up. Besides, I could never rest after conferring with I Mávri Pórta. Too many strange thoughts would swirl around my mind, keeping me up. I mopped blood and slime instead. At dawn I drove to The Black Door and met with Benny Dickson. The place was empty at that hour expect for prep cooks and Gregory. My waiter brought me to Benny’s office. He glanced at the filled Cambro and smiled. “This is good.” Benny sat behind his clean desk, hands folded under his chin. “I’m glad you had a change of heart, Sam. I really am.” I placed the Cambro on his desk. He tapped the lid. “All for me?” “I owed you.” Frowning, he said, “You did.” He licked his lips, drummed his fingers on the edge of the container. “You won’t try to quit again. Won’t try to keep the ambrosia for yourself.” This was not a question. I laughed a single, dry huff. Quit. Like it was a job. Like I had a choice. “No.” “How was the girl I got you?” My throat tightened. “She didn’t listen.” Benny ran his finger along the edge of the container, his eyes fixed on the lumpy, contents. “That’s your fuck up. Don’t blame me if you don’t prep them properly.” “Yeah.” My guts twisted. He wasn’t wrong, but I was on the clock. “My fault.” Benny stood and peered into the green-black filth. He always tested the product. He couldn’t help himself. “Go ahead.” A faint expression of suspicion crossed his features, but he was already on the hook. He dipped his hand into the muck, searching for the crystalline orbs. “Cheers.” The ambrosia flashed red and blue. Benny gasped and tried to pull his hand away, but it was too late. His screams filled the office as I Mávri Pórta wrapped around his hand and arm. He bucked and fell into the chair, taking the siphonophore with him. It wriggled along his chest, the float bladder quivering with movement, the rubbery wings pulsating, the tendrils flashing hunter lights and injecting toxins. “What did you do?” His voice was already half gone, echoing from another place. “Freedom comes at a price. It’s time for you to see Hell for yourself.” “You son of a—” He flickered and I saw his veins and tendons. His ocular cavities and bones. I didn’t care. I was more scar tissue than man by then, and this was the only way out. “Bye, Benny.” He came back long enough for me to see the terror in his eyes. This man who’d saved me only to enslave me. I was done being a guinea pig. Done being afraid. But as Benny disappeared from the world, I realized there was one thing I wasn’t done with. I was an explorer. A journeyman. And finally, I had the key to the black door all to myself. Afterword A t the risk of hyperbole, I believe those of us who love the macabre know best what it's like to not fit in. It's the age-old question of causality, of course. Do
